planting.note: Scarify seeds before direct sowing in fall into rocky, calcareous, well-drained soil.

directions: Talus Scurfpea is a native perennial of rocky limestone talus slopes, calcareous dry prairies, and dry hills from Kansas south through Texas. It produces palmately compound leaves covered in resin dots and dense, upright spikes of blue-violet pea flowers in late spring and early summer. Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into very lean, rocky, or gravelly, well-drained calcareous soil in full sun. Once established, it is highly drought-tolerant and develops a deep, starchy tuberous root; avoid disturbing or transplanting.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into well-drained, calcareous to neutral rocky or sandy soil.

directions: Palmleaf Scurfpea is a low-growing native perennial of dry, rocky, calcareous prairies and hillsides in Texas and Oklahoma, notable for its palmately compound leaves with five to seven spreading, finger-like leaflets that give the species its name. In spring it produces dense, short spikes of blue to purple pea flowers above silvery-hairy, mat-forming clumps. Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into very well-drained, calcareous to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun. It is an excellent choice for limestone rock gardens and dry prairie restoration plantings, and is highly drought-tolerant once its deep taproot is established.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into dry, sandy or rocky, calcareous to slightly acidic well-drained soil.

directions: Dwarf Scurfpea is a distinctive, low-growing native perennial of dry, sandy or calcareous open ground in Texas and adjacent south-central states, notable for its unusually low stature and for flowers and seed pods that may develop partly at or below the soil surface — a trait reflected in the name hypogaeum, meaning underground. It forms spreading, mat-like growth with palmately compound leaves and clustered blue to purple pea flowers held close to the ground in spring. Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into dry, well-drained, sandy or rocky, calcareous to slightly acidic soil in full sun. Once established it is extremely drought-tolerant and requires virtually no supplemental irrigation or fertilization.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into dry, well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade.

directions: Sampson's Snakeroot is a native perennial legume of dry, open woods, prairie margins, and rocky or sandy open ground throughout the southeastern and south-central United States. It produces gland-dotted, trifoliate leaves with distinctive purple-black glands visible when held to light, and slender, spike-like racemes of blue-violet pea flowers from May through July that are highly attractive to native bees. Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun to light shade. Drought-tolerant once established, it is an ecologically valuable native legume for dry native gardens, open woodland edges, and dry prairie restoration plantings.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Native Plant Database

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sow into dry, sandy, acidic, nutrient-poor, very well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Smooth Scurfpea is a rare, conservation-priority native perennial legume of dry, open longleaf pine flatwoods and sandy pinelands in the Mississippi-Alabama coastal plain region. It is unusual among legumes in producing simple or unifoliolate leaves rather than compound leaves, a trait encoded in its species name simplex, and bears short, spike-like racemes of blue to purple pea flowers in summer. Grow in dry, sandy, acidic, nutrient-poor, very well-drained soil in full sun, avoiding clay or amended soils that this lean-soil specialist cannot tolerate. Scarify seeds before fall direct sow; source plants or seeds only from reputable native nurseries given its conservation status, and use it in longleaf pine restoration or dry sandhill plantings in zones 6 through 9.
